claimed_contribution: Identifies photo-text content similarity as a novel antecedent of online review helpfulness.
Extends visual-verbal interplay research from stylized advertising settings to large-scale, organic user-generated reviews using machine learning and experiments.
Shows that ease of processing is the mechanism behind the similarity effect and that processing impediments attenuate this effect.
context: The paper studies user-generated restaurant reviews on Yelp, where reviewers post text and optional photos. The main field setting covers Los Angeles County and the Boston area.
design_and_data: Study 1 uses Yelp panel data on 24,964 restaurants, 7.4 million reviews, 3.5 million associated photos, and 2.1 million reviewers from 2004-2020 (2021 for Boston). Photo labels are extracted with Google Vision API, review text and photo-label documents are embedded with Doc2Vec, and cosine similarity is used to measure photo-text content similarity; helpfulness is measured by useful votes. The authors estimate OLS models with restaurant and time fixed effects, rich controls, and robustness checks including reviewer fixed effects, LDA topic controls, and review-to-review similarity, plus interactions with text difficulty (Flesch-Kincaid) and image quality (NIMA). They also validate algorithmic similarity against human judgments (500 MTurk raters) and run five experiments (total N = 3,879) with Yelp-based and designed stimuli to test causality, mediation, and boundary conditions.
main_findings: Reviews with photos receive more useful votes than text-only reviews.
Among reviews with photos, greater photo-text content similarity is associated with and causally increases review helpfulness.

Greater similarity increases helpfulness through higher ease of processing, and this benefit is weakened or eliminated when processing is impeded by difficult language, lower image quality, or disfluent presentation.
one_line_summary: Across Yelp field data and five experiments, reviews with photos are more helpful, and helpfulness rises further when photo and text convey similar content because similarity increases processing ease.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior review-helpfulness and visual-verbal communication literatures by examining the joint role of photos and text in organic user-generated reviews, where prior work largely focused on text features, reviewer traits, or photo presence alone. It introduces ease of processing as a mechanism and shows boundary conditions based on processing difficulty.
research_gap: Prior work on review helpfulness mostly examines textual content, ratings, and reviewer characteristics, while work on visuals typically studies photo presence or image characteristics without incorporating accompanying text. The paper addresses the missing evidence on how photo-text content similarity in user-generated reviews affects helpfulness and why.
research_question: Do photos increase online review helpfulness, and does the degree of content similarity between review photos and text change that effect? If similarity matters, is the effect explained by ease of processing, and does it weaken when processing ease is reduced?
